title: Boundaries
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Boundaries
summary: While the word 'boundaries' does not appear in Scripture, the principle is everywhere — from Nehemiah's walls to Jesus' withdrawals, healthy limits are acts of stewardship, not selfishness.
body:
Nehemiah rebuilt the walls of Jerusalem against fierce opposition. Walls in the ancient world were not about isolation — they were about defining what was inside and what was outside, who had access and who did not [verse:neh.4.6]. Boundaries serve the same function in relationships.

Jesus practiced boundaries consistently. He withdrew from crowds when he needed solitude [verse:luke.5.16]. He declined requests that did not align with his mission [verse:luke.12.13-14]. He set expectations with his own family when their agenda conflicted with his calling [verse:mark.3.33-35]. None of this diminished his love. It protected his capacity to love.

Proverbs provides the clearest boundary metaphor: a person without self-control is like a city broken into and left without walls [verse:prov.25.28]. Without boundaries, everything gets in — including what should not.

Paul sets relational boundaries with the Corinthian church, refusing to visit in a way that would cause pain and instead writing a letter [verse:2cor.2.1-4]. He also instructs the Thessalonians to keep away from anyone living in idleness — not out of hatred but to establish accountability [verse:2thess.3.6].

Boundaries in Scripture are not walls of resentment. They are acts of wisdom that protect both the self and the relationship from patterns that would otherwise destroy both.

verses.annotation: Nehemiah rebuilds Jerusalem's walls — boundaries as protection and definition.
A person without self-control is like a city without walls — vulnerable to everything.
Jesus regularly withdrew for solitude — boundary-setting as spiritual practice.
Jesus declines a request outside his mission — not every demand deserves a yes.
Jesus redefines family according to calling, not obligation.
Paul chooses a letter over a visit to avoid a painful confrontation — wise relational strategy.
Paul instructs distance from the idle — boundaries as accountability, not rejection.

scholars.quote: Longman reads Proverbs 25:28 as a statement about internal governance — the 'walls' in question are a person's own capacity for self-regulation, without which they are defenseless against external and internal threats.

Keener notes that Jesus' regular withdrawals in Luke were not signs of avoidance but strategic rhythms that sustained his capacity for public ministry — rest and solitude were prerequisites for effective engagement.
scholars.source: The IVP Bible Background Commentary: NT (Keener, 1993)

scholars.quote: Fee argues that Paul's decision to write rather than visit the Corinthians in 2 Corinthians 2 was itself a boundary — he chose the communication method least likely to cause mutual harm while still addressing the issue.
scholars.source: The Second Epistle to the Corinthians (Fee, NICNT, 2014)
